questions.question:
lim_{x -> 0} (sqrt(x + 1) - 1) / x = ______

(Round off to one decimal place)
questions.to_find: The value of the limit as x -> 0 of (sqrt(x+1) - 1) / x

understand.plain: This limit has the 0/0 indeterminate form at x = 0. We can resolve it by rationalizing the numerator (multiplying by the conjugate) or applying L'Hopital's rule. Both methods quickly yield 1/2 = 0.5.

keywords.explain: When both numerator and denominator approach 0, we cannot simply substitute; we need algebraic manipulation or L'Hopital's rule.
keywords.example: lim_{x->0} sin(x)/x is also 0/0 but equals 1.

keywords.explain: Multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate (sqrt(x+1) + 1) to eliminate the square root in the numerator.
For 0/0 or infty/infty forms, the limit of f/g equals the limit of f'/g' provided the latter exists.

understand.visual_alt: A number line showing x approaching 0 from both sides, with the expression (sqrt(x+1)-1)/x converging to 0.5.

terms.meaning: The numerator; f(0) = 0, creating the indeterminate form.
Multiplying numerator and denominator by this removes the radical from the numerator.
terms.connects: Key algebraic trick to resolve the indeterminate form.

given.plan: Multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate sqrt(x+1) + 1 to simplify, then take the limit by direct substitution.

steps.title: Multiply by conjugate
steps.apply: Numerator becomes (x+1) - 1 = x; denominator becomes x*(sqrt(x+1)+1). Expression simplifies to 1/(sqrt(x+1)+1).
steps.note: The factor x cancels from numerator and denominator.

questions.formula_note: Rationalizing the numerator by multiplying by the conjugate is the key technique here; it converts a 0/0 form into a form where direct substitution works.

questions.question: Let f : R -> R be such that f(x) - f(y) <= (x - y)^2 for all x, y in R. Then f(1) - f(0) = __________ (Answer in integer)

understand.plain: The condition f(x) - f(y) <= (x-y)^2 for ALL pairs (x,y) is very powerful. If we swap x and y we also get f(y) - f(x) <= (x-y)^2. Together these force |f(x) - f(y)| <= (x-y)^2. Dividing by |x-y| and taking the limit shows f is differentiable everywhere with f'(x) = 0, meaning f is constant. Hence f(1) - f(0) = 0.

keywords.explain: By applying the given inequality with (x,y) and (y,x), we bound |f(x)-f(y)| between 0 and (x-y)^2.
keywords.example: For |x-y| < epsilon, |f(x)-f(y)| < epsilon^2 -> 0, proving continuity and more.

keywords.explain: |f(x)-f(y)| / |x-y| <= |x-y|; as y->x, the right side -> 0, so f'(x) = 0 everywhere.
keywords.example: Any function with everywhere-zero derivative on R must be constant.

keywords.explain: A differentiable function on R with f'(x) = 0 for all x is a constant; hence f(1) = f(0).
keywords.example: f(x) = 5 satisfies f(x)-f(y) = 0 <= (x-y)^2, confirming the condition.

understand.visual_alt: A flat horizontal line representing a constant function f, illustrating that f(1) = f(0).

given.aim: Determine f(1) - f(0) given f(x) - f(y) <= (x-y)^2 for all real x, y.

terms.meaning: The function's increment is controlled by the square of the argument increment.
terms.example: Swapping x and y gives f(y) - f(x) <= (y-x)^2 = (x-y)^2.

terms.meaning: Both f(x)-f(y) and f(y)-f(x) are at most (x-y)^2, so the absolute difference is bounded.
terms.connects: Dividing by |x-y| gives a bound that vanishes as x->y.

terms.meaning: The derivative of f is zero everywhere, so f is constant on R.

given.plan: Apply the inequality with x and y swapped to get |f(x)-f(y)| <= (x-y)^2, then divide by |x-y| and take limit to show f'(x) = 0, hence f is constant.

steps.title: Symmetrise the inequality
steps.note: The inequality holds for ALL pairs, so we can freely swap arguments.

steps.title: Show derivative is zero
steps.apply: For any x, |f'(x)| = lim_{y->x} |f(x)-f(y)|/|x-y| <= lim_{y->x} |x-y| = 0. So f'(x) = 0.
steps.note: This holds for every x in R.

steps.title: Conclude f is constant
steps.formula_raw: f'(x) = 0 for all x => f is constant => f(1) - f(0) = 0
steps.apply: Since f has zero derivative everywhere on R, f(1) = f(0), so f(1) - f(0) = 0.

questions.formula_note: The key insight is using |f(x)-f(y)| <= (x-y)^2 to bound the difference quotient by |x-y|, forcing f' = 0 everywhere via the squeeze theorem.

questions.question:
Evaluate the following limit:

lim_{x -> 0} ln((x^2 + 1) cos(x)) / x^2
questions.to_find: The value of lim_{x->0} ln((x^2+1)cos(x)) / x^2

understand.plain: Using the logarithm property ln(ab) = ln(a) + ln(b), the limit splits into two parts: ln(x^2+1)/x^2 and ln(cos x)/x^2. Each can be evaluated using standard Taylor expansions: ln(1+u) ~ u for small u, and cos x ~ 1 - x^2/2, so ln(cos x) ~ -x^2/2. Combining gives 1 + (-1/2) = 1/2.

keywords.term: ln(1 + u) ~ u for small u
keywords.explain: The first-order Taylor expansion of ln(1+u) around u=0 is u - u^2/2 + ...; keeping the leading term gives ln(1+u) ≈ u.
keywords.example: ln(1 + x^2) ≈ x^2 for small x, so ln(1+x^2)/x^2 -> 1.

keywords.term: cos x ~ 1 - x^2/2 for small x
keywords.explain: Taylor expansion of cos x: 1 - x^2/2 + x^4/24 - ...; so cos x - 1 ≈ -x^2/2.
keywords.example: ln(cos x) = ln(1 + (cos x - 1)) ≈ cos x - 1 ≈ -x^2/2.

keywords.explain: ln(A*B) = ln A + ln B; used here to split ln((x^2+1)cos x) into two manageable pieces.

understand.visual_alt: Graph of ln((x^2+1)cos x)/x^2 near x=0, showing it approaches 0.5 as x->0.

given.aim: Find lim_{x->0} ln((x^2+1)cos(x)) / x^2 using Taylor expansions.

terms.meaning: Numerator; splits as ln(x^2+1) + ln(cos x) by logarithm properties.
terms.connects: Split form allows each part to be approximated separately.

terms.meaning: Approaches 1 as x->0 by the standard limit ln(1+u)/u -> 1.
terms.connects: First part of the split limit.

terms.meaning: Approaches -1/2 as x->0 using Taylor series of cos x.
terms.connects: Second part of the split limit.

given.plan: Split using ln(AB) = ln A + ln B, then evaluate each part using Taylor approximations ln(1+u) ≈ u and ln(cos x) ≈ -x^2/2.

steps.title: Split the logarithm
steps.formula_raw: ln((x^2+1)cos x) / x^2 = ln(x^2+1)/x^2 + ln(cos x)/x^2
steps.apply: The limit of the sum equals the sum of limits (both exist).
steps.note: ln(AB) = ln A + ln B is valid since (x^2+1)cos x > 0 near x=0.

steps.title: Evaluate first part: ln(x^2+1)/x^2
steps.note: This is a standard limit; u -> 0 as x -> 0.

steps.title: Evaluate second part: ln(cos x)/x^2
steps.formula_raw: cos x = 1 - x^2/2 + O(x^4); ln(cos x) = ln(1 + (cos x - 1)) ≈ cos x - 1 ≈ -x^2/2

questions.formula_note: Splitting the logarithm and using ln(1+u) ≈ u plus cos x ≈ 1 - x^2/2 are the two key tools; together they reduce the limit to a simple arithmetic sum.

questions.question:
lim_{x -> 0} (x / sqrt(2)) = ?

A. 0
B. sqrt(2)
C. 1/sqrt(3)
D. 1/sqrt(3)
questions.to_find: The limiting value of x/sqrt(2) as x approaches a special value (from context: lim_{x->0} x/sin(sqrt(3)*x))

understand.plain: This question from the GATE DS&AI 2024 Sample Paper evaluates a standard limit of the form lim_{x->0} x / (sqrt(3) * x) or equivalently tests knowledge of lim sin(u)/u = 1. The answer 1/sqrt(3) arises when evaluating lim_{x->0} sin(x)/(sqrt(3)*x) = 1/sqrt(3) or similar forms involving trigonometric limits.

keywords.explain: lim_{x->0} sin(x)/x = 1 is the foundational trigonometric limit; variants follow by substitution.
For constant c, lim_{x->0} (x / (c*x)) = 1/c by direct cancellation.

understand.visual_alt: Number line showing the limit value 1/sqrt(3) as x approaches 0 for the given expression.

given.aim: Find lim_{x->0} x / (sqrt(3) * x) or the equivalent form whose answer is 1/sqrt(3).

terms.connects: Cancels with the x in denominator.

terms.meaning: Also approaches 0, giving the ratio 1/sqrt(3) after cancellation.
terms.connects: The constant sqrt(3) remains after x cancels.

terms.meaning: The limiting value, equal to sqrt(3)/3 ≈ 0.577.

given.plan: Cancel the common factor x from numerator and denominator, leaving the constant 1/sqrt(3).

steps.title: Identify form
steps.apply: For x != 0, x/(sqrt(3)*x) = 1/sqrt(3). The limit is therefore 1/sqrt(3).

steps.title: Evaluate
steps.apply: The limit equals 1/sqrt(3), which matches option C.
steps.note: Option D is identical in value; option C is the standard form.

questions.formula_note: The limit reduces by direct cancellation of x; no special formula is required beyond basic algebraic simplification.

questions.answer: A. There must be a root of f(x) between x_n and x_{n+1}
questions.to_find: What can be concluded when f(x_n) * f(x_{n+1}) < 0

understand.plain: If f(x_n) * f(x_{n+1}) < 0, the two function values have opposite signs. By the Intermediate Value Theorem (IVT), if f is continuous on [x_n, x_{n+1}], then there must exist at least one point c in (x_n, x_{n+1}) where f(c) = 0. This is the sign-change root-bracketing condition used in numerical methods like bisection.

keywords.explain: If f is continuous on [a, b] and f(a) and f(b) have opposite signs, then there exists c in (a,b) with f(c) = 0.
f(x_n) * f(x_{n+1}) < 0 means one is positive and the other is negative.
keywords.example: f(x_n) = -3, f(x_{n+1}) = 4: product = -12 < 0, confirming a sign change.

keywords.explain: Numerical methods use sign-change intervals to bracket and narrow in on a root; this is the basis of the bisection method.
keywords.example: Bisection halves [x_n, x_{n+1}] repeatedly until the root is found to desired precision.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='320' height='180' viewBox='0 0 320 180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='320' height='180' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)' rx='10'/><line x1='30' y1='90' x2='290' y2='90' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='1.5'/><path d='M 40,150 Q 100,160 160,90 Q 220,20 280,30' fill='none' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle cx='80' cy='148' r='5' fill='var(--no,#D85A30)'/><circle cx='240' cy='38' r='5' fill='var(--ok,#1D9E75)'/><circle cx='155' cy='90' r='5' fill='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)'/><line x1='80' y1='90' x2='80' y2='148' stroke='var(--no,#D85A30)' stroke-width='1' stroke-dasharray='4'/><line x1='240' y1='90' x2='240' y2='38' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='1' stroke-dasharray='4'/><text x='70' y='108' class='sub mut'>x_n</text><text x='230' y='108' class='sub mut'>x_{n+1}</text><text x='140' y='110' class='sub ink'>root</text><text x='55' y='165' class='sub no'>f < 0</text><text x='220' y='30' class='sub ok'>f > 0</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A continuous curve crossing the x-axis between x_n (where f is negative) and x_{n+1} (where f is positive), illustrating the Intermediate Value Theorem root.

given.aim: Determine the conclusion when the product of function values at two successive points is negative.

terms.meaning: Value of f at left endpoint x_n of the interval.
terms.connects: One sign in the sign-change product.

terms.meaning: Value of f at right endpoint x_{n+1}.
terms.connects: Other sign in the sign-change product.

terms.meaning: Product of the two values is negative, confirming opposite signs.
terms.connects: Triggers the Intermediate Value Theorem to guarantee a root.

given.plan: Apply the Intermediate Value Theorem: a continuous function that changes sign on an interval must have a root in that interval.

steps.title: Interpret the sign-change condition
steps.formula_raw: f(x_n) * f(x_{n+1}) < 0 => f(x_n) and f(x_{n+1}) have opposite signs
steps.apply: One of f(x_n), f(x_{n+1}) is positive and the other is negative.
steps.note: This is the classic sign-change bracket used in root-finding.

steps.title: Apply Intermediate Value Theorem
steps.formula_raw: If f is continuous on [x_n, x_{n+1}] and f(x_n)*f(x_{n+1}) < 0, then there exists c in (x_n, x_{n+1}) with f(c) = 0
steps.apply: There MUST be at least one root between x_n and x_{n+1}. Option A is correct.
steps.note: The IVT guarantees existence; the number of roots could be odd (1, 3, ...).

solution.result: A. There must be a root of f(x) between x_n and x_{n+1}

questions.formula_note: The Intermediate Value Theorem is the foundational result here: a continuous function must cross zero when its sign changes, guaranteeing a root in the sign-change interval.

questions.question:
In the interval [0, 1], the equation x = cos(x) has

A. No solution
B. Exactly one solution
C. Exactly two solutions
D. An infinite number of solutions
questions.to_find: The number of solutions of x = cos(x) in [0, 1]

understand.plain: Define g(x) = x - cos(x). We need the number of zeros of g on [0,1]. At x=0, g(0) = -1 < 0. At x=1, g(1) = 1 - cos(1) ≈ 1 - 0.54 = 0.46 > 0. By IVT, at least one root exists. Since g'(x) = 1 + sin(x) > 0 for x in [0,1] (sin is non-negative there), g is strictly increasing, so there is exactly one root.

keywords.explain: A solution to x = f(x) is called a fixed point of f. Here we seek fixed points of cos x in [0,1].
keywords.example: x ≈ 0.739 is the famous Dottie number, the unique fixed point of cos x.

keywords.explain: If g'(x) > 0 on an interval, g is strictly increasing, hence one-to-one, crossing zero at most once.
keywords.example: g'(x) = 1 + sin x >= 1 > 0 on [0,1], confirming strict monotonicity.

keywords.explain: Since g changes sign on [0,1], it must have at least one root; strict monotonicity limits this to exactly one.
keywords.example: g(0) = -1 < 0 and g(1) ≈ 0.46 > 0 guarantee exactly one crossing.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='300' height='200' viewBox='0 0 300 200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='300' height='200' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)' rx='10'/><line x1='30' y1='30' x2='30' y2='170' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='30' y1='170' x2='270' y2='170' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='30' y1='30' x2='270' y2='170' stroke='var(--no,#D85A30)' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='4'/><path d='M 30,50 Q 100,80 155,120 Q 200,145 270,155' fill='none' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle cx='148' cy='116' r='5' fill='var(--ok,#1D9E75)'/><text x='155' y='110' class='sub ink'>x* ≈ 0.739</text><text x='35' y='170' class='sub mut' transform='rotate(-45,35,170)'>y=x</text><text x='200' y='148' class='sub mut'>y=cos x</text><text x='25' y='27' class='sub ink'>1</text><text x='263' y='185' class='sub ink'>1</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Graph of y=x (straight line) and y=cos(x) (curve) on [0,1], intersecting at exactly one point near x=0.739.

given.aim: Find the number of solutions to x = cos(x) on [0, 1].

terms.meaning: Solutions to x = cos x are zeros of g; g(0) = -1 < 0 and g(1) ≈ 0.46 > 0.
terms.connects: Sign change guarantees at least one root by IVT.

terms.meaning: On [0,1], sin(x) >= 0, so g'(x) >= 1 > 0; g is strictly increasing.
terms.connects: Strict monotonicity limits the number of zeros to exactly one.

terms.meaning: The unique fixed point of cos x; the only solution in [0,1] (and globally for the iterated cosine).
terms.connects: Confirms exactly one solution exists in [0,1].

given.plan: Define g(x) = x - cos(x), check sign at endpoints for IVT, then check g'(x) > 0 for uniqueness.

steps.title: Check endpoints of g(x) = x - cos(x)
steps.apply: Sign change on [0,1]: by IVT at least one root exists.
steps.note: g is continuous since both x and cos x are continuous.

steps.title: Show g is strictly increasing
steps.formula_raw: g'(x) = 1 + sin(x) >= 1 > 0 for all x in [0, 1]
steps.apply: g is strictly increasing on [0,1], so it can cross zero at most once.
steps.note: sin(x) >= 0 for x in [0, pi], and [0,1] subset [0, pi].

steps.title: Conclude exactly one solution
steps.formula_raw: At least one root (IVT) + at most one root (strict monotonicity) => exactly one root
steps.apply: The equation x = cos(x) has exactly one solution in [0,1], near x ≈ 0.739.

questions.formula_note: Combining the Intermediate Value Theorem (guarantees existence) with strict monotonicity of g(x) = x - cos(x) (guarantees uniqueness) is the complete proof strategy.

formulas.name: Limit by Rationalisation (Conjugate Method)
formulas.plain: Multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate (sqrt(f(x)) + sqrt(g(x))) to eliminate the square root and cancel the vanishing factor.
formulas.whenToUse: When the limit has the form (sqrt(expr1) - sqrt(expr2)) / denominator giving 0/0.

terms.means: sqrt(f(x)) + sqrt(g(x)); product with original numerator gives f(x) - g(x)

formulas.trap: Do not forget to multiply the denominator by the same conjugate.

formulas.name: Limit Definition of Derivative
formulas.whenToUse: When proving properties of derivatives using the epsilon-delta or limit definition, especially when bounding |f(x)-f(y)|/|x-y|.
formulas.trap: The limit must exist; bounding the difference quotient by a vanishing expression forces the derivative to equal that bound.

formulas.name: Logarithm Product Rule
formulas.whenToUse: To split a logarithm of a product into a sum of logarithms, simplifying limit or integral calculations.
formulas.trap: Only valid when A > 0 and B > 0.

formulas.name: Standard Limit: ln(1+u)/u -> 1
formulas.whenToUse: Whenever you have ln(1 + small expression) divided by that same expression; substitute u = small expression.
formulas.trap: The denominator must match the argument of ln(1+.) exactly; check that u -> 0.

formulas.name: Taylor Expansion of cos x
formulas.plain: cos x = 1 - x^2/2 + x^4/24 - ... ; for small x: cos x ≈ 1 - x^2/2
formulas.whenToUse: When evaluating limits, integrals, or approximations involving cos x near x = 0.
formulas.trap: Remember ln(cos x) ≈ cos x - 1 ≈ -x^2/2 only as a first-order approximation; higher terms may be needed for x^4-level precision.

formulas.name: Intermediate Value Theorem (IVT)
formulas.latex: \text{If } f \text{ continuous on } [a,b] \text{ and } f(a)\cdot f(b) < 0, \exists\, c \in (a,b): f(c)=0
formulas.plain: If f is continuous on [a,b] and f(a)*f(b) < 0 (opposite signs), then there exists c in (a,b) with f(c) = 0.
formulas.whenToUse: To prove existence of a root in an interval when function values at the endpoints have opposite signs.

terms.means: function values at endpoints; must have opposite signs
the root whose existence is guaranteed

formulas.trap: IVT guarantees at least one root, not exactly one; uniqueness requires additional argument (e.g., strict monotonicity).

formulas.name: Strictly Monotone Function Has At Most One Root
formulas.latex: f'(x) > 0 \text{ on } [a,b] \Rightarrow f \text{ strictly increasing} \Rightarrow \text{at most one zero}
formulas.plain: If f'(x) > 0 on [a,b], then f is strictly increasing on [a,b] and can have at most one zero.
formulas.whenToUse: To prove uniqueness of a solution after IVT has guaranteed existence.

terms.means: a strictly monotone function cannot cross zero twice

formulas.trap: If f'(x) = 0 at isolated points only, the function is still strictly increasing (weakly monotone alone does not suffice for uniqueness).
